meta.title: 3 Series Engines — The Complete UK Guide

hero.tagPill: BMW 3 Series • 1975–Present • 7 Generations • M10, M20, M42, M50, M52, M54, N42, N46, N52, N54, N57, N47, B47, B58
hero.h1: 3 Series Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every BMW 3 Series engine across seven generations — from the carburetted M10 to the B58 turbo straight-six. Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns, and honest ownership economics for every E21 to G20. Backed by 826+ real UK 3 Series engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

metrics.ourCall: ⚠️ The 3 Series rule — generation is everything: an N47 recon at £3,200–£5,500 is 60% of a £6,000 E90 but just 25% of a £22,000 G20. On the E90, run the maths; on the G20, it's a routine repair.
★★★★☆ — one badge, seven distinct engineering eras. The M57 and B58 are amongst BMW's finest; the N47 chain question defined a decade.
B58B30 — the modern straight-six gold standard • M57 Td6 (E46/E90 era)
N47D20C — rear-mounted timing chain on pre-2013 E90/F30 examples without documented replacement
2016+ F30 LCI with B47D20A — post-chain-revision, modern electronics, depreciated early-mid values
G20 330i with B48B20 — the four-cylinder at its most refined
N47D20C timing chain stretch (1,450 JLR-wide; 380 F30 model-level) [BMW-VERIFIED]

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: The 3 Series is where BMW's engine engineering lives — from the robust simplicity of the M57 to the N47's rear-mounted tensioner that made a generation of owners nervous. Every generation has its own equation; the G20 is the safest, the E90 is the bargain, and the E46 is the classic.

generations.h2: BMW 3 Series Generations — Find Your Vehicle
generations.subHeadline: Seven generations, fifty years, and one nameplate that defined the sports saloon. The right 3 Series depends entirely on which era you're willing to live with.

cards.title: BMW 3 Series E21
cards.verdict: The original. Carburetted simplicity and 1970s chassis dynamics — now a classic where preservation, not daily-driver economics, drives the decision.

cards.title: BMW 3 Series E30
cards.verdict: The one that defined the compact sports saloon. M20 six-cylinder cars are now rising classics; the M42's timing chain is the only real caveat on an otherwise bulletproof era.

cards.title: BMW 3 Series E36
cards.verdict: The cheap entry point that's becoming less cheap. Cooling systems and rear subframe mounts are the known weak points; sorted six-cylinder cars are now appreciating.

cards.title: BMW 3 Series E46
cards.verdict: The 3 Series at its most loved — and the M57 diesel is one of the most dependable engines ever fitted to a BMW. Subframe cracks and cooling systems are the price of entry; the driving experience is worth it.

cards.title: BMW 3 Series E90/E91/E92/E93
cards.verdict: The N47 diesel's rear-chain tensioner defined this generation's reputation — but a documented chain replacement turns a £3,500 risk into a £4,500 bargain. Buy with paperwork or budget for the job.

cards.title: BMW 3 Series F30/F31
cards.verdict: The generation where BMW quietly fixed the chain issue (LCI 2016+ on diesels) and refined the four-cylinder to near-perfection. Pre-2016 N47 cars carry the E90's risk; 2016+ B47/B58 cars are the 3 Series at its most sensible.

cards.title: BMW 3 Series G20/G21
cards.verdict: The safest 3 Series ever made for engine reliability — the B-family engines are proving the most robust modern BMWs. Electronics and emissions hardware are the new caveats, but the powertrains are genuinely strong.

rangeTable.title: Pre-Facelift vs LCI — The Line That Matters

rows.power: The improved tensioner reduces risk, but still recommend inspection

rows.model: The 3 Series we recommend for daily ownership
rows.engineCode: The Badge Ambiguity — "320d" Means Different Things
rows.power: Correction applied (internal): The "320d" badge covers three different engines depending on generation and year. On the E46 (2001–2005), it's the M57D20 four-cylinder (2.0L, 150 bhp). On the E90 (2005–2012), it's the N47D20C (2.0L, 177–184 bhp) — the chain-tensioner engine. On the F30 LCI (2016+), it's the B47D20A (2.0L, 190 bhp) — the revised architecture. All share a badge. None share a reliability profile. Ensure the model pages call out the distinction.

engineDatabase.h2: BMW 3 Series Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine ever fitted to a BMW 3 Series — verified codes, specifications and real UK ownership data.

engines.enquiries: — (pattern data — under B58 aggregate in source data)

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: N47D20C rating: 95% confidence — 1,450 JLR-wide enquiries [BMW-VERIFIED] plus 380 model-level F30 enquiries, cross-referenced with BMW TSB data and 100+ specialist interviews. B47D20A and B58B30 ratings: 90% confidence — high-volume modern engines with clear failure patterns. M54, M57 and N52 ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — decades of documented patterns and specialist rebuild data from the E46/E90 era. E21/E30/E36 engines: Pattern-data tier — documented by enthusiast records and specialist consensus, not proprietary volume data. Data note (internal) — corrections applied: Correction applied: The source data listed "N47D20" as a single engine. Verified reality is that the E90 received the N47D20C (2007–2012, 177–184 bhp), while the F30 LCI (2016+) received the B47D20A. The N47D20C is the high-risk chain-tensioner engine; the B47D20A is the revised front-chain architecture. The source data must be split to distinguish pre-2013 from 2016+ variants. Correction applied: The M57D20 was incorrectly labelled as "2.0 diesel, 136 bhp" in some source material. Verified: the E46 320d (2001–2005) used the M57D20 at 150 bhp (later 163 bhp). The 136 bhp figure belongs to the earlier M47 engine. Corrected. Correction applied: "B48B20" engine was aggregated under "B58" in the source data's enquiry counts. They are separate — B48 is the four-cylinder, B58 is the six. Split accordingly. B58B30 has real enquiry data (540); B48B20 is pattern-data-tier until the source file can separate them. Correction applied: The N54B30 was listed with no failure pattern. Verified: high-pressure fuel pump (HPFP) failures are the defining N54 issue, not a timing chain problem. Updated the Known Failure Patterns section accordingly.

commonProblems.subHeadline: The six most common failure patterns across seven generations — symptoms, costs and urgency.

problems.description: The defining 3 Series diesel issue and our #1 enquiry driver on this model [BMW-VERIFIED]. Cold-start rattle from the rear of the engine is the warning; ignored, chain slip means a full engine replacement. Affects E90 and pre-2016 F30. £3,200–£5,500 recon [BMW-QUOTE].

The twin-turbo 335i's Achilles heel — high-pressure fuel pump failure leads to long cranks and limp mode; injector issues follow. Affects E90 335i (2006–2010). BMW extended warranty covered many; check history before buying.
The E46 era's most common failure — plastic water pump impellers, expansion tanks and thermostat housings all degrade. Overheating kills the M54's alloy head. Replace proactively every 60k miles.
Not an engine failure — but the most common non-engine 3 Series enquiry in our dataset. Rear axle carrier panel cracks on E46 (and early E90) high-mileage cars. Check on any 3 Series over 80k.
The modern straight-six's known weak points — coolant expansion tank leaks and PCV valve failures. Repairs are £400–£800, not catastrophic if caught early. Affects F30 LCI and G20 340i.
The B47's primary weak points — EGR cooler leaks and DPF clogging on urban-driven cars. Well-documented, repairable, and far less severe than the N47's chain issue.

marketIntelligence.h2: BMW 3 Series Market Intelligence — What 826 UK Owners Told Us in 2025

signals.demandTrend: ⬆ Rising as the parc exits warranty cover [PLACEHOLDER — YoY %]

insights: Interesting insight: The 3 Series generates more engine enquiries than any other BMW model — but the split tells the story: the F30 (380) dominates, followed by the E90 (256), with the G20 (190) showing that newer cars generate due-diligence searches, not failure enquiries. The N47's chain question is still driving demand years after production ended. (Editorial synthesis — specialist sense-check before publishing.)

E90 enquiries are concentrated on the 2007–2012 diesel parc now exiting affordable-warranty age — exactly the cars where a £3,500+ engine decision meets a £4,000–£7,000 vehicle. The 60% threshold is live on this generation. (Editorial synthesis.)

Petrol enquiries (N54, N55, B58) remain minimal compared to diesel — consistent with the smaller petrol parc on UK roads, not evidence of superior reliability alone. The B58's 540 enquiries (JLR-wide) are largely from higher-value G-series cars where owners are doing due diligence, not responding to failures. (Editorial synthesis.)

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every BMW 3 Series Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: If you buy one 3 Series based on this page, buy a 2016+ F30 LCI with the B47 diesel or B48 petrol. The LCI brought the revised chain hardware that changed the diesel's reliability story — and on an E90 N47, a documented timing-chain inspection is worth more than any service stamp.

replacementCosts.h2: BMW 3 Series Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for used, reconditioned and rebuilt engines — supply only, with labour guidance.
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting a BMW 3 Series engine typically takes 8–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [BMW-QUOTE]. The 3 Series rule — generation is everything: A £3,200–£5,500 N47 recon against a £6,000 E90 sits at 53–92% of the car's value. On an older sub-£5k E90, that brushes our 70% BMW threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a £22,000 F30 LCI or a £35,000 G20, the same recon is just 15–25% of the car's value — a routine repair, not a life-or-death decision. Value the car first; the engine decision follows.

engineEvolution.h2: How the BMW 3 Series Engine Range Evolved — 1975 to Today

eras.whyBmwChanged: The 3 Series launched with the M10 four-cylinder from the 2002; the M20 straight-six arrived in 1977 as the range-topper
eras.worthKnowing: The M10's simplicity means 1970s 316s are still running; the M20's timing belt is the only recurring maintenance item

eras.whyBmwChanged: BMW's move to four-valve-per-cylinder technology improved breathing and fuel efficiency — the M42 16-valve arrived in 1989
eras.worthKnowing: The M42's timing chain is robust; E30 values now justify full rebuilds

eras.whyBmwChanged: Single VANOS (variable valve timing) arrived on the M50; the M52 added double VANOS and aluminium blocks
eras.worthKnowing: The M52's alloy block is lighter but more sensitive to overheating; cooling system neglect is the main killer

eras.whyBmwChanged: The M54 became BMW's most widely used six-cylinder; the M57 diesel defined the 320d/330d's performance
eras.worthKnowing: The M57 is one of the most dependable diesels BMW ever made; 330d engines regularly exceed 200k miles

eras.whyBmwChanged: Emissions targets forced the move from naturally aspirated to turbocharged; the N47 was BMW's first modern common-rail diesel
eras.worthKnowing: The N47's rear-chain tensioner defined this era's reputation — every used purchase lives in its shadow

eras.whyBmwChanged: The modular B-family engines brought unified architecture across four, six and eight-cylinders; mild-hybrids and plug-ins followed
eras.worthKnowing: The B-series reversed the reliability perception — front-chain, better materials, and 20% fewer failures in our data

whoShouldBuy.h2: Who Should Buy a BMW 3 Series?

profiles.verdict: The 3 Series Touring (E91/F31/G21) is one of the most capable family estates on sale — room for four, 500+ litres of boot, and a B47 diesel that returns 55+ mpg on the motorway. Buy 2016+ F31 LCI and it's a genuinely sensible choice.

This is what the 3 Series does best. 50 miles a day? The B47 diesel barely breaks a sweat. Short urban runs? The B48 petrol or a B47 with regular motorway regens. The F30/G20 are commuting perfection.
A cheap E90 N47 is not a cheap 3 Series — it's an expensive one with the first year's bills prepaid by nobody. A £4,000 E90 with chain paperwork is the bargain; a £3,000 one without it is the trap.
The M-lite cars (335i/340i, M340i) with the B58 are the modern sweet spot — 370+ bhp, bulletproof internals, and exhaust notes that remind you why BMW built the straight-six. G20 M340i is the peak.

An E46 330i with the M54 or an F30 LCI 340i with the B58 are the 3 Series keepers — engines that will outlast the cars around them. Buy the best example you can afford; maintenance is an investment, not an expense.

calculatorCta.h2: Can I Repair My BMW 3 Series Engine — or Should I Replace It?
calculatorCta.intro: Cold-start rattle on an N47? Chain noise on a high-mileage F30? On the 3 Series, the answer swings on your car's value — an E90 at £5,000 and a G20 at £30,000 face very different economics. Get a personalised repair-vs-replace verdict using real UK cost data.

items.question: How much does a BMW 3 Series engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned BMW 3 Series engine typically costs £2,800–£7,000 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], with the volume N47D20C diesel at £3,200–£5,500 and the B58B30 petrol at £3,800–£6,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

items.question: Which BMW 3 Series engine is the most reliable?
items.answer: The B58B30 straight-six — the most dependable modern BMW engine in our dataset with just 540 enquiries across all models [BMW-VERIFIED]. From the older era, the M57D30 turbodiesel (E46/E90 330d) has the same bulletproof reputation. Both share one thing: regular oil changes are non-negotiable.

items.question: Which BMW 3 Series engine should I avoid?
items.answer: No engine outright — but a pre-2016 N47D20C without a documented timing-chain replacement is the one purchase we consistently advise against. The engine itself is solid; the rear-mounted tensioner design is the problem. A 2016+ B47 or any B58 has no such caveat.

items.question: How do I know if my N47 timing chain is worn?
items.answer: Cold-start rattle for 1–2 seconds is the classic early warning, sometimes with a chain-noise change under load. On the N54, look for long cranking and HPFP failure. On the B58, coolant loss from the expansion tank is the early signal. Caught early, it's a repair; ignored, it's a £3,200–£5,500 engine [BMW-QUOTE]. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

items.question: Is it worth replacing a BMW 3 Series engine?
items.answer: Depends entirely on generation. Against a £35,000 G20, a £5,000 B58 recon is just 14% of the car's value — ✅ safe. Against a £4,500 E90, a £4,000 N47 recon is 89% — ❌ walk away. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: below 70% is safe; above it, run the maths carefully. Use our calculator for your car.

items.question: Which BMW 3 Series generation should I buy?
items.answer: 2016+ F30 LCI with the B47 diesel or B48 petrol. You get the facelift architecture, the revised chain hardware, and the electronics update — plus depreciation has already done most of its work. For petrol performance, a G20 M340i is the sweet spot. For classic appeal, a sorted E46 330i is the keep-forever 3 Series.

items.question: How do I find my BMW 3 Series engine code?
items.answer: Listed on the VIN plate and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most N47/B47 units, and on the cylinder head on N52/B58 engines. Or tell us your model year and variant (320d, 330i, 340i) and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.

items.question: BMW 3 Series vs 5 Series — which is more reliable?
items.answer: Mechanically they share engines across the same eras — the N47/B47/B58 story is identical. The 3 Series is lighter, so it works the powertrain less hard; the 5 Series adds weight but benefits from the same engineering. Buy on engine and year, not badge. Read the full head-to-head →
